speaker: interviewee
text: So, at midnight, at midnight, all of a sudden I see a small house, and light-- some-- some light in the house. I went there, and-- and I looked into-- I looked into the-- into the window. I can see an old woman. She is singing. Singing psalms, you know? I-- I knocked at-- at their window, and she asked, who is it? And I said, please, mother, open the door, you'll see who is it. She opened the door, and she saw me, and I-- I tell her the story. And I was with her about three weeks. Nevertheless, she doesn't have anything to eat. So every morning, I-- I woke up every morning, go to the field, and dig-- dig out some potatoes, frozen potatoes, and frozen beets, and that was our food. OK, now, the next-- next episode. OK, let's leave. The next episode happened to me in July, July 1942, when I went further to the east. So I went to a small village. And the village was full of Germans. And I was looking how-- how to fled from here. But I don't know where to go. And I can see an old man, sitting outside his home. I went to him, but I didn't see he show me, don't approach, don't approach. But I am there. At the same very moment, a German soldier in his underwear-- a red one, I remember him like that-- with the red eyes. He jumped out, took me here, and pushed me into the house. There were two more soldiers drinking there. And they were all drunk. And he took his gun, and said to them, [GERMAN]. I'll kill him. He's Jewish. I'll kill him. And they said-- they-- they started to laugh at him. They didn't tell anything, they started to laugh on him. But at the same very moment the old man come in, and he started to talk to them. He said, it's my relative. He lives nearby, at the-- at the next village. It's my relatives. You know, and-- and I was lucky again. But after that, I couldn't walk. [CHUCKLES] I couldn't walk. And I walked away, and I fell down on the field, fell asleep. And again, I was sick. And it was the same thing. OK. And now the last episode. It was-- so I walked further, again, to the Don River. At the one side of the Don River, at the west side, there have been the Germans. At the east side, the Red Army. And I was dreaming again to cross the river and to join the Red Army. But it was impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came up to a small village, and a woman was outside. And she said, Come in. I wasn't the only one, you know. People like me used to wandering. So I approach her. She said, Are you hungry? I was always hungry. She gave me borscht, she gave me fried bread, I remember, fresh warm bread, she gave me. But she didn't look like a peasant, you know? I saw she's from a city, but I didn't ask question. And so-- and so she-- And then, after that, she gave me a piece of bread, and said to me, Are you Jewish? I said no. She said, OK. But be careful here. There are a lot of here, Germans, police, and Hungarians. And even the Hungarians, they-- they are worse than the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went only 100 feet. A Hungarian soldier grabbed me. And I-- she-- and she saw that. And she was shaking her head, like that. So he gave me to the police. And the policeman was the chief of police, a teacher, he was. I was told he was a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he made some investigation, we are, you are against [INAUDIBLE]. I lie, I start to lie, I am [? there. ?] I am Ivan. [INAUDIBLE]. OK. Now he filled out some papers. You're going to the camp. And there was a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the camp at the city of Alexeyevka. At this camp, I heard people were dying, hundreds every day from hunger. So-- but, plus, I am Jewish. So they-- they wouldn't let me into the camp. They killed me before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, this guy will convey you to-- to the camp. It's eight kilometers from here. OK. But on the way, we just left the-- the village, he was a young guy, 17 years old. I said, do you have a father? Yes. Where is your father? He is in the army. And then I ask him, You tell me, if you got to-- to take your father to the camp, would you? He said no. So why-- why should you took me to the camp? And he said, But I am afraid. I am a policeman.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to him, shoot two times, in the air, and I'll escape. You'll tell them I escaped. And he did it. He shoot two times in the air. And I-- and I really ran, and ran and ran and ran into the field. And find, again, a stack of hay, and crawled in, and was there for two days. Only the hunger, you know, sent me out from there. And then I went, again, back. And this was the less sharp episode. I settled just in a-- in a village, and did some work. Until then, in the winter, I joined the partisans for a short times. The partisans we organize them, themselves. And we present a lot of Italians, and some-- some Jews. And we killed 50 SS men there. Yes, and that's what-- after-- after that I went back to join the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before-- before you tell me about that, I just want to back up a minute. All these little villages you were in, and you were hidden in, do you know the names of any of the villages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last village was Bolbot. This is the village where I settled for-- in August 1943, '42, till February 1943-- after Stalingrad. After, you know, the famous battle in Stalingrad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell-- tell me about, you were going by the name of Ivan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you decided on that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided very simply. Because when I was a-- a kid, when you used to live at home, a neighbor of mine, his name was Ivan [? Dyomov ?] and that's, you know, it's easy to remember for me. That was easy. And that's why. It's a-- a simple explanation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you start using that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the very first moment when I took the boat. Remember, I took the boat? And the other guy joined me. I started to use this name. I was prepared. I was preparing myself before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you the partisan group. How did you join the partisans, how did that come to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was, you know, after Stalingrad. When the German army, the Italian army, and the Hungarian, they started to retreat. And then, the police was nothing, you know? We didn't fear the police. We did not afraid of the police. And then we joined-- at the same-- at the village, Bolbot there have been over 100 prisoners like-- like me. So that we did. We joined the army. We joined the-- the partisan group.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was been in charge of that group? Who was the leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a leader, an official. I don't remember his name. It was a leader. But it wasn't, you know, a famous group like that, because it exists only for-- for two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was part of the group? What kind of people were part of this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Former soldiers who escaped prison, like me. Just former soldiers who escaped prison. They were-- and at the same village, I knew there was two more Jews. Two more. But we didn't communicate with each other. We didn't talk to each other. But I knew about it. And I want to tell you a last episode, that was when I came to join the army, we got to go to a city of Valuyki. It's in Russia. It's not Ukraine. It's in Russia. And they took us all to a school. And from that school, they gave us the directions where I have to go to the city of Khar'kov. The other guys have to go to another city to join the army. We [? share ?] our battle there on the front. So we-- we got to sleep at the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the second day, they call-- they called us all to the KGB. It was the KGB-- headquarter of the KGB. And they got to check us, you know, because we have been at the occupation zone of the Germans. Who are we?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I anticipate bad things, because I knew, if you are Jewish, they always said you couldn't survive. You should be killed. But if you survived, so you collaborated with the Germans. You got to be tried. You've got to go to jail. You know what it was. And I knew that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, it was a row in the corridor of the school. It was a row, maybe 50 people there. I went to the very end. It passed a few minutes, and I saw a Jewish guy went out from the room, by two people, by two convoys. And he was arrested. So, when I got in, I didn't say my real name. I said just, I am Ivan [? Dyomov, ?] and it took them just a few minutes. And I get out. But when I came, it was city of Merefa where the company was. I should-- I should to be with this company. Then I said my name, my last name, my real last name, but not the first name. I said Mikail Becker. And that's-- and that was the start of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, like I said, I show you the map, like that. So I was in battles-- in very, very bad battles. But I survived. And I was never afraid for my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, you weren't afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, no. And I ended up the war in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me some of the battles you were in? And describe what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you. Three times, after that my unit, we are encircled again. You know, again in-- in the castle. But that time I was smart, and I managed to escape. Not only me. I lead some other soldiers. The first time I lead 30 soldiers with me. And we escaped. But how we escaped. We escaped in March. In March, going through-- through the-- through a lake. And we were wet after that, until we joined again the army for two days. And I was not sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other [INAUDIBLE] sharp moment, was in Hungaria, The same thing. And that's-- I mentioned my friend, it was the [? old ?] friend, his picture. He was a very good friend. He was Ukrainian. Very good friend of mine. We were encircled, you know. And at that time, at that point, I was afraid. I'm thinking, it's the end. It's almost the end of the war, and I-- and I got to die. Because if the Germans will come, they encircled us. So it's the only way. I will die. They'll shoot me. And it was at the middle of the night. And we were nervous, all of us. We didn't sleep. He used to-- to work, you know, for the-- for the Chief of Staff of the battalion. And he got all the papers, all the documents. And he came up to me, and gave me a blank book, you know, identification book. But it's blank. He said, Keep it. And I understood him. If I will be imprisoned, I fill out, again, [? Dyomov ?] or some other name. So that was, you know, a very good friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Ivan Schwitz. That was his name. And we-- we met after the war. But he died a long time ago. He was much older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you said it-- that you knew it was almost the end of the war. How did you know that the war was coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because we were on offensive now, not on defensive, on offensive. We came up, can you imagine, from the Don River, near the Don River-- I'll show you on the map later. From the Don River, we came up to the-- to the Carpath, to Hungarian, to-- to almost-- to Hungarian, to Poland. We were sure. Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember any of the names of the commanders of this unit, that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of this unit, I don't remember. I knew he was an architect in civil life. But, oh, yes, Kryznosolov. He was, yeah, Major Kryznosolov. He was a good man. Intelligent man. And we were-- we have been friends with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the second time that you were in the army, did your-- the other soldiers know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yes. You know, and they used to say, you are good Jewish. The other Jews are bad, but you're a good Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't have any ill treatment by these men?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could treat myself, you know, I saw always we were better have to go. We are-- where are my friends? Who is my friend? And who is my enemy?

speaker: interviewer
text: During all of this time, did you have any idea what had happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not have. But I was-- actually, I was aware. I did not know. I was aware what happened to my family, what happened to some other groups, you know? We went back, you know, at the offensive through [? Shtatlak ?] and no-- no one is there. No Jews. Judenrein. You know, how the Germans say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have any contact at all with any of your family members during all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They have been evacuated in Siberia-- to Siberia, and to the Asian part of Russia. So two families. My niece was there, and my brother's wife with her daughter in Siberia. Their daughter, I mentioned Nelly. That's-- that's her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe what happened, how you felt, when the war ended. Where were you when-- when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria. In Austria. Well, happy, happy, and happy. Well, despite I know I have no family anymore. But you know, still happy. And I met some Jews in Hungary, and they offered me, let's go, let's go. We'll took you to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, I was afraid, you know? Because I could be-- if I will be caught, I could be shot at the same moment, by the-- by the Russian army. And then, I don't know why. But it just was probably, maybe some of my family is there still. I knew they are not, but maybe. And I went back. What were you doing in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was very sorry. I was very regret after that. Why didn't I go? Why didn't I go?

speaker: interviewer
text: To Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to-- on duty. I was there on duty. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you said something about Hungary. When did you-- was this also why you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's altogether, through-- We went to Austria through Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe to me the events that happened after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I still was in the army. The war ended up in May 1945. I was still in the army till June 1946. So I was dismissed then. And I went-- I had the privilege to-- to choose a city, because I was in the war. So I had the privilege, for privilege. And I choose Kiev, where my brother used to live there, and where I used to live there. And I came up, and I found-- find work-- found work, and started to work. But it was-- it was very, very harsh life, a very hard life there. No food. And the-- the money you got is maybe for one day. It's less for one day, so it was very hard. But after that, you are still work. And it passed a-- a couple of years. Then I went back to school, to engineering school. So after six years studying there, I got an engineer, structure engineer. And I worked all my life, till I immigrated to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me your wife's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Rita. Actually, I met her in 1946, and we married in 1949. But it wasn't a girlfriend like now. [LAUGHING] You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you couldn't live together until you married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have some medals. You have some photographs of-- pictures of medals. When did you receive those?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- on the way, you know, of the offensive. On the way of the offensive, and the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the reasons that you received the medals?

speaker: interviewee
text: For participating in this battle or this battle, in this battle or this battle. In capturing this city or this city. So I got them, I think, seven or eight medals. And one very important medal. They called it Orden of Red Star. So it is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me just briefly what life was like for you, living in Kiev, and staying in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you used to live, you know, bad or worse, you used to live. But it wasn't good. It wasn't good like-- like I live in here. I will tell you. We used to live in one room for many years, three generations-- my-- my wife's parents, my wife and I, and my children. You can't imagine this, can you? [CHUCKLES] For many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you had thoughts of coming to the United States before you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I was, let me tell you, the-- the communist government was our enemies, my enemies, and I was their enemies. Since they destroyed the Jewish culture, and that happened in 1948, so they-- they closed the only one Yiddish theater in Moscow-- no, in Chernowitz. They called-- they closed the only one newspaper, in Moscow. They arrested, you know, the-- the Jewish writers. 30 people of Jewish writers. And that was all. They finished with the Jewish culture. And then I became an enemy of them. Despite I-- I was in the war, you know, but still. And I was dreaming of immigrating from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of your family members live in-- in the Soviet Union as well, besides yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, right after the war. What I want to know is, what happened to your family? Can you tell us what happened to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes. So my brother, who survived the war, he lived in Kishinev. It's also Soviet Union, but it's Bessarabia, you know? He lived there with his family. My two nieces, they lived in Chernowitz with their family. And they-- they passed away there, but the son of one nieces, he is in New York now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names is [? Jenny ?] and [? Basia ?] are the names. And now, my brother-- my brother Josef-- he was in Kiev. And he died in 1950-- in 1961. He died. And his family was there. And my niece Nelly, which I mentioned before, she died in, five or six years ago. I got a letter from there. She died. Her son, he lives now in Israel. So actually I don't have relatives anymore there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many of your brothers and sisters were-- were killed during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brothers, one brother and his family. Two children and a wife. I-- I don't remember if I tell you how he was tortured. He was tortured. He was stabbed. And that's what I was told. And he-- he lied on the street three, two or three days. He was begging them, kill me, kill me, and no one killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: David. He was, at the time, 36. His wife was killed. One, the older son, he was about 12, I think. He managed to escape, and wandering over the villages. And the-- the police were just the Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE] there. But I've got all so many relatives. And altogether, I don't know, you can account maybe 50, maybe more.

speaker: interviewer
text: 50, maybe more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 50, maybe more. They were killed. All of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was killed at Yom Kippur. He was 80 years old. In 1946, after I was dismissed from the army, pretty soon I came to the village, to Piliava, to see. I didn't recognize the village. There was no homes. No home, nothing there. But my home, can you imagine, my home was there. Because it's not in the shtetl, it's in the village. It was occupied by a Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a neighbor, who knows my father, she knew me, she told me, she saw them at Yom Kippur. She saw him. So he rode a wagon. He was dressed in a tallis, because it was Yom Kippur. And, yeah, and he said goodbye to them. And he was killed that day. They shot him. So it had been all them-- all of them. But one guy survived. I saw him then. He survived. He was there, you know? He was there, and somehow he survived in the nighttime. He get out and a peasant in a village, she saved his life. So before you came to the United States, what were you doing in Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working as a design engineer, structure, design engineer. And that was my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take before you were able to immigrate to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I got to tell you. In 1977, we visited Milwaukee. United States and Milwaukee. Because my sister, she was writing and begging me, come in, come in. Otherwise, we will not meet now [CHUCKLES] at this life. So before that, I was afraid, you know? I was afraid to illegal to say I have relatives. I never said I have relatives in the United States. Otherwise I couldn't work. But at that time, I was 61, and the retirement in Russia was 60 years old. So I thought, it's OK. Now I have to tell them I'm going to the United States. You want-- want to dismiss me, whatever you want to do. You want to fire me, fire me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, I got an invitation. And my wife and I, we came here. We visited Milwaukee. We got a real nice time for six weeks here. We went back. And then at the very moment, I decided we got to go. It's time to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, before that, I dreamed to go to Israel. But the relatives here, you know, sisters and others, they said, look, come here. And then we got to go. So my-- my daughter, she came first here. Have six months earlier she came here. And then in-- we left the Soviet Union in May 1979. And we settled here in Milwaukee in September 20, 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] tape.
